coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescence probe using Brown HT precursor carbon dots for highly selective and ultrasensitive quantitation of potassium ion in some staple food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Esmail, Lawen Abdulsatar
Othman, Hazha Omar
Omar, Nigar Abdulkareem
Jabbar, Hijran Sanaan
coredata.dc:description: Potassium ions (K+) perform a variety of functions in biological systems, including the regulation of blood pressure, enzyme activation, maintenance of muscular strength, regulation of extracellular osmolarity and facilitation of nerve transmission. Thus, a one-step hydrothermal-assisted method was used to prepare small-sized photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) from Chocolate Brown HT food dye for the first time. The resulting CDs exhibited strong blue emission, with sizes ranging from 1.8 to 3.2 nm (average 2.5 nm) and an estimated quantum yield of 42.76 %. The complex functional groups present on the surface of the CDs enhance their affinity for certain ions. Leveraging the effective fluorescence quenching effect of K+, we developed a highly sensitive CD-based fluorescence analytical system with a limit of detection of 0.32 nmol/L. Linearity was established between 1.0 nmol/L and 400 nmol/L. Furthermore, K+ detection in staple food samples was accomplished using this sensor. The use of 1200 nmol/L sodium ions as an interfering agent resulted in an error of less than 5 %. To validate the accuracy of the proposed method, the results were compared to those obtained using flame atomic emission spectroscopy as a reference method, yielding recovery values ranging from 97.69 % to 103.19 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chocolate Brown food dye
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Potassium ion
Staple food

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Temperature effect; (b) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of CDs (inset: FCDs solution under daylight (left) and ultraviolet light (right)); (c) Effect of time on the synthesis of FCDs; (d) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs excited from 300 to 400 nm; Characterization of synthesized CDs using (e) XRD; (f) EDX; (g) FTIR of CDs; and (h) FTIR of Brown HT. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a, b) HR-TEM images of CDs; (c) size distribution histogram; (d, e) SEM images; and (f) SAED of the synthesized FCDs.

Effect of (a) amount of CDs; (b) pH; and (c) incubation time on the proposed fluorescence sensor; (d) Fluorescence response intensities of the CDs at different K+ concentrations; (e) Calibration curve of the fluorescence quenching value versus K+ concentration; and (f) Effect of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the new CDs probe.
Quenching mechanism of Brown HT-CDs' fluorescence by K+. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison of the proposed CD-probe with published fluorescence sensors for K
+ detection.
Analysis of food samples for K+ using the proposed fluorescence probe and flame atomic emission spectroscopy (FAES).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescence probe using Brown HT precursor carbon dots for highly selective and ultrasensitive quantitation of potassium ion in some staple food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Salahaddin University-Erbil, Erbil, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center, Knowledge University, Kirkuk Road, 44001 Erbil, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center, Knowledge University, Kirkuk Road, 44001 Erbil, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Potassium ions (K+) perform a variety of functions in biological systems, including the regulation of blood pressure, enzyme activation, maintenance of muscular strength, regulation of extracellular osmolarity and facilitation of nerve transmission. Thus, a one-step hydrothermal-assisted method was used to prepare small-sized photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) from Chocolate Brown HT food dye for the first time. The resulting CDs exhibited strong blue emission, with sizes ranging from 1.8 to 3.2 nm (average 2.5 nm) and an estimated quantum yield of 42.76 %. The complex functional groups present on the surface of the CDs enhance their affinity for certain ions. Leveraging the effective fluorescence quenching effect of K+, we developed a highly sensitive CD-based fluorescence analytical system with a limit of detection of 0.32 nmol/L. Linearity was established between 1.0 nmol/L and 400 nmol/L. Furthermore, K+ detection in staple food samples was accomplished using this sensor. The use of 1200 nmol/L sodium ions as an interfering agent resulted in an error of less than 5 %. To validate the accuracy of the proposed method, the results were compared to those obtained using flame atomic emission spectroscopy as a reference method, yielding recovery values ranging from 97.69 % to 103.19 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Potassium ions (K+) play a crucial role in various biological processes. The functions of K+ in these processes include blood pressure regulation, enzyme activation, maintenance of muscular strength, extracellular osmolarity and nerve transmission (He, Mortellaro, Leiner, Fraatz, & Tusa, 2003). Depending on age, the typical range of potassium concentration in plasma is between 3.5 and 5.5 mmol/L (Kjeldsen & Schmidt, 2019). Disorders of potassium balance can lead to hyperkalaemia or hypokalaemia (McDonald, 1995). Several diseases are associated with abnormal K+ concentrations (X. Zhou et al., 2011). Consequently, sensitive and selective K+-sensors are essential for facilitating diagnosis and treatment through the detection of potassium levels. Various analytical methods have been developed for determining potassium concentration. These methods include spectrophotometry (B. Zhou, Zhang, Zhang, Zhang, & Jiang, 2007), atomic absorption spectrophotometry (de Jesus, Silva, & Vale, 2008), electrochemical method (Ge, Huang, Sen, & Yu, 2010), atomic emission spectrometry (Barros, de Oliveira, de Magalhães, & Villa, 2012) and chromatography (Fan et al., 2012). However, these methods are often associated with complex, expensive instruments and are time-consuming, which limits their practical applications. Furthermore, some methods exhibit relatively high limits of detection, narrow detection ranges and a lack of high selectivity and sensitivity (Zhang, Chen, Zhao, & Huang, 2015).

Fluorescence probes have recently attracted attention owing to their simplicity, superior sensitivity—approximately 1000 times greater than that of other analytical optical methods—ease of use and high selectivity (Saberi, Rezaei, & Khayamian, 2018). Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) are a type of nanomaterial, within the carbon family characterized by their mechanical, electronic, chemical, optical and thermal properties (Esmail & Jabbar, 2023b). Researchers are increasingly interested in carbon dots (CDs) owing to their blinking luminescence, compatibility with various materials, flexibility in surface modification and low toxicity. These attributes render CDs highly attractive for applications such as photochemical reactions (De & Karak, 2017), electrochemical processes and the detection of ions and biomolecules (Huang et al., 2015). Doping CDs with sulfur, nitrogen or other heteroatoms enhances their ability to bind analytes by providing additional binding sites (Abdulsatar Esmail & Sanaan Jabbar, 2023). Owing to their photoluminescent properties, carbon nanoparticles can serve as chemo- or bio-sensors (Bourlinos et al., 2008). Furthermore, their straightforward synthesis procedures and ease of functionalization contribute to their broad applicability. A simple one-step hydrothermal method was employed by Yin and colleagues (Yin et al., 2024) to prepare an efficient ‘on-off-on’ fluorescent sensor based on chiral CDs. The chiral CDs, utilizing D-tryptophan and L-tryptophan as chiral sources were designated as DCDs and LCDs, respectively. Potassium ferricyanide effectively quenched the bright fluorescence of the D/L CDs.

Brown HT (chocolate Brown HT) is a dyestuff known by its IUPAC name, disodium 4,4′-(2,4-dihydroxy-5-hydroxymethyl-1,3-phenylene bis-azo)di-(naphthalene-1-sulfonate). It is also referred to in the literature as CI 20285, E 155 and food brown 3 (Karimi et al., 2022). Brown HT, with the molecular formula C27H18N4Na2O9S2, is a synthetic azo dye that appears as a brown powder. It is commonly used as a dye additive in various food products, including soft drinks, sauces, ice cream, puddings, canned meat, cakes, confectionery and flour (Karimi et al., 2022).

The development of an assay that can selectively and sensitively detect K+ is essential. Thus, photoluminescent CDs of small size were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method with Brown HT. Potassium ions effectively quench the fluorescence of these CDs, allowing them to serve as a fluorescence probe for the sensitive detection and quantification of potassium levels in food samples without any pre-treatment. The results obtained were compared to those produced by flame photometry as a reference method.

All compounds used in this study were of analytical grade, allowing for direct application without the need for purification. Deionized water was utilized throughout all experiments. Brown HT E155 was sourced from Glentham LifeScience® (CAS RN: 4553-89-3) (Glentham Life Science, Corsham SN13 9SW, United Kingdom). Other chemicals were procured from Sigma Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany), including CrCl3·6H2O (97 %,), CoCl2·6H2O (98 %,), KCl (99.9 %), AgNO3 (99.9 %), MgSO4·7H2O (99.5 %), Pb(NO3)2 (99 %), CuCl2.2H2O (99 %), NiCl2·6H2O (98 %) and CaCl2 (90 %). FeCl3.6H2O (98 %), FeCl2.9H2O (98.5 %) and ZnCl2 (97 %) were obtained from Fluka (Fluka Chemie AG, Buchs, Switzerland). Nitric acid (ACS Reagent, density 1.42, 70 %) was procured from Honeywell Fluka (Vantaa, Finland). KCl salt was used to prepare a K+ stock solution (1.0 mM), and to achieve lower concentrations, the stock solution was serially diluted.

HR-TEM images were captured using a PHILIPS CM120 transmission electron microscope (Eindhoven, Netherlands). A PFP7 flame photometer (Jenway, United Kingdom) was used as the reference method. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using an IR-Affinity-1 SHIMADZU spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) at 25 °C. UV–visible spectra were obtained using an LKB BIOCHROM 4050/4051 spectrophotometer (Biochrom, Cambridge, England). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired with a TESCAN MIRA (III) (Czech Republic). Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) was conducted using an EDX 7200 (SHIMADZU, Kyoto, Japan) system attached to the SEM. Fluorescence measurements were performed with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF 6000) (SHIMADZU, Kyoto, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Philips PW 1730 instrument (Netherlands).

As a precursor, Brown HT, a commercial product, was used to prepare the FCDs using a modified hydrothermal carbonization method. In 50 mL of deionized water, 0.05 g of Brown HT was completely dissolved with stirring. Hydrothermal treatment was carried out for 5 h at 200 °C after transferring the mixture into a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave. Centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min was performed after the mixture naturally cooled to room temperature. The supernatant was filtered through a micro-syringe filter with a diameter of 0.20 μm (Lv, Man, Dong, Huang, & Wang, 2020).

The experiments were conducted at room temperature and normal pressure. A total of 100 μL (1 mg/mL) of CDs solution and KCl solution (with concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 400 nmol/L) were separately added to a 5 mL volumetric flask. Phosphate buffer at pH 7 was used to dilute the mixture. After mixing for 5.0 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission wavelength of 420 nm, using a 10 nm slit width (Wang et al., 2021). To test selectivity, 20 μL of various metal ions (Ag+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Cr3+ Mg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and K+) were added to the CD solutions and the fluorescence quenching was compared.

In accordance with established procedures, the quantum yield (Qu.Yi.) of the synthesized CDs was determined. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (Qu.Yi. of 0.54 at 360 nm) served as the standard. During excitation at 360 nm, absorbances in the 1 cm fluorescence cuvette were maintained below 0.05 to minimize the re-absorption effect. To calculate the Qu.Yi. of the CDs, the following equation was used:
Qu . Yi . =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2

In this equation, (I) represents the measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, (A) denotes the absorbance and (n) signifies the refractive index of the solvent. Additionally, (R) indicates the corresponding parameter of a known fluorescent standard.

Randomly selected food samples from a specific brand were collected from a market in Erbil City, Iraq, weighing either 0.5 kg or 1.0 kg. An analytical mill was employed to grind the samples after they were weighed. A quantity of 0.1 g of the samples was accurately measured and placed in 50 mL polyethylene vials, to which 5 mL of 70 % nitric acid was added. The samples were then subjected to decomposition in an ultrasonic bath with heating for two hours at 80 °C, ensuring that the lid was partially open to relieve pressure and mitigate the risk of explosion during the process. After cooling the solution to room temperature, the flask was filled with deionized water to a total volume of 250 mL for subsequent analysis (de Oliveira, Antunes, Vieira, Medina, & Ribeiro, 2016). A volume of 100 μL of the final solution (or a serial dilution for high concentrations of potassium) was utilized for the determination of K+ in accordance with the procedure outlined in section 2.4.

The flame photometer was maintained with a flow rate of 3–6 mL/min and a continuous air supply of 15 to 30 psi at 6.0 L/min. A standard stock solution of potassium with a concentration of 200 nmol/L was used to calibrate the flame photometer. The flame performance was further optimized by adjusting other instrumental parameters. The samples were examined following instrumental calibration.

The only precursor used in CD synthesis is Brown HT. The synthesized CDs resulted in a yellowish-brown solution upon dispersion in water. The effects of time and temperature on the hydrothermal carbonization reaction of Brown HT were investigated to enhance the quality and performance of the CDs.

To determine the best synthesis conditions, CDs were synthesized at various temperatures (160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C and 220 °C). Each synthesized CD solution was characterized using UV–visible spectra. According to Fig. 1a, 200 °C is the optimum temperature for FCD synthesis. As the temperature increased from 160 °C to 200 °C, the CD spectra exhibited two peaks at 250 nm and 315 nm, similar to those reported by Alarfaj and co-authors (Alarfaj, El-Tohamy, & Oraby, 2018). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1b, UV (365 nm) illumination revealed intense blue luminescence. Thus, 200 °C was selected as the optimum temperature.

During CD synthesis, it is imperative to verify that the conversion of Brown HT into CDs has been completed under experimental conditions before separating the final products. This verification resulted in the observation of a continuous conversion process at 200 °C. As shown in Fig. 1c, the conversion ratio is low after 3 h. At five hours, the conversion reaches its highest point, indicating that all biomass-convertibles have been converted into CDs. However, after extending the reaction time to 10 h, carbonization begins to decrease somewhat. Prolonged reaction periods can cause the functional groups of CDs to break down (Esmail, Jabbar, & Salih, 2024; Hola et al., 2017).

As shown in inset Fig. 1c, the CD solution appears brownish transparent under daylight illumination, while it emits blue fluorescence under UV illumination. UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy were used to study the electronic and optical properties of the CDs. Two absorption bands are identified in Fig. 1c corresponding to the π–π* and n–π* transitions of the CO and CC groups in the synthesized CDs, centred at 250 nm and 315 nm, respectively (Alarfaj et al., 2018).

It is observed that the excitation wavelength depends on the fluorescence characteristics. This phenomenon arises from variations in particle sizes, emissive domain distributions and surface emissive traps. The fluorescence spectra of CDs are illustrated by changing the excitation wavelength. Fig. 1d shows the fluorescence spectra of CDs as the excitation wavelengths change from 300 nm to 400 nm. When excited with a wavelength of 350 nm, a strong fluorescence emission peak was observed at 420 nm (Saberi et al., 2018).

In Fig. 1e, the XRD spectrum is presented. The sharp peak at approximately 27° corresponds to the (002) peak of graphite, indicating that the CDs possess a graphitic structure (Wu et al., 2018). The EDX analysis shows that the elemental composition of the FCDs includes 25.09 % carbon, 32.23 % nitrogen, 41.46 % oxygen, 0.50 % sodium and 0.72 % sulfur, as depicted in Fig. 1f. The elemental analysis of the synthesized CDs reveals that they primarily consist of oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur and carbon atoms.

Based on the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1 g, the absorption values at 3649.32 and 1662.64 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO and –OH stretching modes of the -COOH groups, while the peak at 3210 cm−1 is due to NH stretching vibration. The peak around 1331 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of CN, and NH bending vibrations are associated with the band at 740 cm−1. Furthermore, CH and CC vibrations are evident at 2922.16 cm−1 and 1541.12 cm−1, respectively, when compared with the Brown HT spectra (Fig. 1h). The peak at 1120 cm−1 is attributed to SO stretching vibration, and absorption at 1330.88 cm−1 identifies the C-O-C bond (Saberi et al., 2018).

In accordance with previously published studies, we determined the quantum yield by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities of standard quinine sulfate with those of the synthesized Brown HT-CDs. The CDs exhibited a strong blue emission, with sizes ranging from 1.8 to 3.2 nm (average size: 2.5 nm), and an estimated quantum yield of 42.76 %. The decay lifetime of the blue-emitting CD fluorescence was measured to be 9.2 ns.

As-synthesized CDs were identified using various HR-TEM images, and size distributions were calculated using ImageJ software. Fig. 2 a,b show HR-TEM images of the CDs, which are well-dispersed without aggregation and have a roughly spherical shape (Lu et al., 2016). According to the histogram in Fig. 2 c, particle sizes range from 1.8 to 3.2 nm, with an average diameter of 2.5 nm. Additionally, SEM images in Fig. 2 d,e indicate that the CDs appear roughly spherical. Fig. 2 f presents the SAED pattern for the CDs, where the diffused ring pattern suggests the presence of amorphous carbon.

Fluorescence sensors depend on specific experimental conditions to be effective. The concentration of CDs, pH and incubation time for K+ binding were optimized to achieve the best sensing performance. Various CD concentrations were tested to assess the quenching performance. By increasing the CD amount from 50 to 100 μL, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs rose from 535 to 964. However, when the CD concentration was increased from 100 μL to 200 μL, the fluorescence intensity decreased due to self-quenching of the CDs. Therefore, 100 μL (1 mg/mL) was selected as the optimal concentration of CDs (Fig. 3a).

In addition, the fluorescence quenching of CDs by 250 nmol/L K+ was studied at different pH values. A pH adjustment was made using PBS buffer (0.10 mol/L), and the mixed solution was incubated for five minutes. Fig. 3b shows an increase in the Fo/F ratio with respect to pH, reaching its maximum value at pH 7; however, further increases in pH resulted in a decrease in the Fo/F ratio. Consequently, additional experiments were conducted at a pH of 7 for the solution. This finding is consistent with previously published articles on CD fluorescence, which were prepared from thioctic acid (Wang et al., 2021), and amaranth dye (E123) (Esmail & Jabbar, 2023a).

In the presence of 250 nmol/L K+ and an optimal concentration of CD, we evaluated the time-dependent fluorescence quenching of the probe. Fluorescence quenching was found to be most effective at 5 min (Fig. 3c). Consequently, the quenching kinetics suggest that K+ quickly interacts with the probe (Saberi et al., 2018).

The fluorescence of CD solutions containing various concentrations of K+ was measured under optimized conditions to construct a standard curve for K+ detection. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence of the CDs is effectively quenched by the K+ solution after its addition, occurring at 420 nm without any blue or redshifts. With increasing K+ concentrations of K+, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 420 nm gradually decreased, reaching a minimum intensity at 400 nmol/L.

Fig. 3e illustrates the linearity of the CD solution for K+ detection, with Fo/F values plotted against K+ concentrations. It is evident that the calibration graph demonstrates a linear relationship between the Fo/F ratios and K+ concentrations across the range of 1 to 400 nmol/L, showing a determination coefficient of 0.9987. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, the linear regression can be expressed as Fo/F = 0.0117C + 1.0321, where Fo and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of K+, respectively, and C denotes the concentration of K+. The limit of detection (L.O·D = 3S.D.b/K) was determined to be 0.320 nmol/L, calculated using the slope of the quenching efficiency curve (K) and the standard deviation of the blank solution (S.D.b) (n = 6).

The approach's accuracy and precision were statistically examined to ascertain the competency of the chemo-sensor, as shown in Table 1 . Using the ideal conditions obtained, five repeated measurements of three K+ standard solutions (50, 150 and 300 nmol/L) were conducted to evaluate the relative standard deviation (RSD) as a measure of precision. Table S1 presents the results of calculating the relative recovery (R %), which was used to assess the method's accuracy. The recovery values ranged from 96.20 % to 99.57 %.

A comparison of the current method and previously published fluorescent K+ detection probes is presented in Table 1. The positive results indicate the high precision and accuracy of the established methodology. This sensing method offers a wider linear range, lower detection limits and higher sensitivity and provides relatively rapid results compared to other reported fluorescence methodologies for measuring K+.

An experimental study was conducted to assess the selectivity of the fluorescence probe concerning K+. To evaluate the fluorescence signals of several interfering metal ions—specifically Ca2+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and 100 nmol/L K+ was added to the CDs solution. The results are presented in Fig. 3 f. The effect of each metal on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was determined independently using 1000 nmol/L of each interfering compound, both with and without the addition of 100 nmol/L K+. The influence of the other ions, apart from K+, can be disregarded. Only K+ exhibited a significant quenching of fluorescence when compared to the other interfering species, as illustrated in Fig. 4 .

The effect of natural food colorants present in staple foods such as black sticky rice and red corn on the quenching of CD fluorescence was reported. The results show that using 80 nmol/L of corn extract anthocyanins has an effect of approximately 4.84 % on the fluorescence intensity. In contrast, using 115 nmol/L of black rice extract anthocyanins results in an effect of about 4.08 % on the fluorescence intensity. Consequently, the FCDs probe demonstrates remarkable selectivity toward K+ compared to other metal ions. Using 1200 nmol/L of sodium ions leads to an error of less than 5.0 %. Based on the results, only K+ was found to be significant.

There is a wide variety of potassium content in staple foods and their preparations. Rice, as a staple food, has a low potassium concentration of (1039 ± 58) mg/kg, while potatoes have a high potassium concentration of (22,900 ± 1056) mg/kg. Around the world, 20 % of food energy comes from rice, while corn and potatoes contribute 5 % and 2 %, respectively. Among processed soy products, tempeh has a higher potassium concentration compared to tofu. Fig. S1a shows the potassium concentrations in staple foods and their derivatives (As'ari, Yusuf, & Mulyaningsih, 2020).

Table S2 shows the daily consumption of various foodstuffs among Asians (As'ari et al., 2020). Some foods have relatively high consumption rates and also contain significant amounts of potassium, including tempeh, shrimp, fish, meat, chicken and rice. However, several foodstuffs have low consumption levels but relatively high potassium concentrations, such as long beans, spinach, water spinach, Chinese cabbage, tomatoes, cassava leaves, cassava, sweet potatoes, potatoes and other vegetables. Therefore, it is important to monitor potassium intake by considering both the food combinations and the potassium concentration in each, such as by increasing vegetable consumption.

The food samples were successfully tested under the optimal conditions of the developed method for determining K+. The diluted sample solutions were analyzed both before and after being spiked with a known concentration of K+. The results of the real sample analysis are presented in The hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize CDs. An investigation was conducted into the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of K+. In comparison to previously reported sensors, this fluorescence sensor demonstrates stability and acceptable analytical performance, including a defined linear range. A comparative analysis between the unique, larger-sized CDs and other CDs indicates that subtle differences in the functional groups on the surface of CDs synthesized from Brown HT, which contain nitrogen and sulfur atoms, contribute significantly to the high efficiency and sensitivity of this fluorescent probe for the selective detection of K+. The potassium levels determined using this method were closely comparable to those obtained via flame photometry. The proposed method offers several advantages: it necessitates only minimal sample quantities and, unlike other related sensors, does not require modified designs, biomolecular modification or sophisticated instrumentation. The proposed CD-based sensor is user-friendly, cost-effective and straightforward.

Table 2 and Fig. S1b. These results were compared with those obtained using flame atomic emission spectroscopy (FAES) as the reference method. The findings indicate that the developed method and the reference method were in good agreement, suggesting that the probe can be used to analyze real samples.

The hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize CDs. An investigation was conducted into the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of K+. In comparison to previously reported sensors, this fluorescence sensor demonstrates stability and acceptable analytical performance, including a defined linear range. A comparative analysis between the unique, larger-sized CDs and other CDs indicates that subtle differences in the functional groups on the surface of CDs synthesized from Brown HT, which contain nitrogen and sulfur atoms, contribute significantly to the high efficiency and sensitivity of this fluorescent probe for the selective detection of K+. The potassium levels determined using this method were closely comparable to those obtained via flame photometry. The proposed method offers several advantages: it necessitates only minimal sample quantities and, unlike other related sensors, does not require modified designs, biomolecular modification or sophisticated instrumentation. The proposed CD-based sensor is user-friendly, cost-effective and straightforward.
This article did not receive any funding.

Lawen Abdulsatar Esmail: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software. Hazha Omar Othman: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nigar Abdulkareem Omar: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Hijran Sanaan Jabbar: Writing – original draft, Resources, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.142341.
